task: R6-C — Healthcare practice state privacy add-on (Pennsylvania patient records + BPINA)
disclaimer: Operational compliance research — not legal advice. BPINA primary statute text not harvestable at palegis consolidated index (2026 chpt renumber); AG official summary used as secondary.

cited_facts.text: A patient or designee (including attorney) has the right of access to medical charts and records and to obtain photocopies without a subpoena duces tecum for the patient's own use.
A patient whose records are copied under the medical-records subpoena subchapter may seek a protective order denying, restricting, or limiting access to copies or originals.

Sealed medical-record copies may be opened only upon direction of the tribunal at trial, deposition, or hearing after confirming the records were subpoenaed or the patient (or authorized representative) consented.

data_security_breach.label: 73 P.S. §2301 — Breach of Personal Information Notification Act (BPINA)

regulator.agency: Pennsylvania Office of Attorney General — BPINA

cited_facts.text: BPINA defines a breach as unauthorized access and acquisition of computerized data that materially compromises security or confidentiality of personal information and causes or is reasonably believed to cause loss or injury to a Pennsylvania resident.

citation.source: PA OAG — BPINA breach definition (73 P.S. §2301 summary)

cited_facts.text: Entities maintaining computerized personal information must notify affected Pennsylvania residents without unreasonable delay after determining information was accessed and acquired in readable form by an unauthorized person.

citation.source: PA OAG — BPINA individual notice requirements

cited_facts.text: When more than 500 Pennsylvania residents are affected, the entity must notify the Attorney General at the same time as individual notice, including breach summary and impacted counts.

citation.source: PA OAG — BPINA Attorney General notice (500+ residents)
